# ENF

> ENF (Event Notation Format) is an event-first communication notation for
> ordered protocol messages over WebSocket, TCP, IPC, event buses, and logs. It
> is a compact protocol format, not a general-purpose programming language or a
> drop-in replacement for JSON.

## Source of Truth

- [docs/specification.md](./docs/specification.md) — normative ENF 1.0
  specification. Grammar, value semantics, errors, limits, and canonical form
  are defined here.

The specification takes precedence over README examples and implementation
details. Models MUST NOT infer grammar rules from source code when the
specification defines the behavior.

## Documentation

- [README.md](./README.md) — installation, quick start, runtime examples, API,
  CLI, limits, and security guidance.
- [package.json](./package.json) — package metadata, exports, engines, and
  development commands.

## Implementation

- [src/index.js](./src/index.js) — JavaScript reference implementation's public
  entry point and exports.
- The library is ESM-only, has no runtime dependencies, and targets modern
  browsers, Node.js, and Deno.
- Public operations include `parse`, `tryParse`, `stringify`, `format`,
  `DEFAULT_LIMITS`, and the ENF error classes.

## Conformance

- [conformance/valid](./conformance/valid) — valid ENF fixtures.
- [conformance/invalid](./conformance/invalid) — invalid ENF fixtures and
  expected error codes.
- [conformance/canonical](./conformance/canonical) — canonical formatting
  fixtures.

These fixtures are implementation-independent JSON test data.

## CLI

- [bin/enf.js](./bin/enf.js) — Node.js-only CLI wrapper around the library.
- Supported commands are `enf check` and `enf format`, including
  `enf format --write` and stdin input.
- The CLI requires Node.js 18 or newer and uses Node filesystem/process APIs.
  It is not the browser or Deno library API.

## Important Constraints

- Every statement MUST end with `;`; newlines do not terminate statements.
- Event names are ASCII, case-sensitive namespace names matching
  `^[a-z][a-z0-9_]*(?:\.[a-z][a-z0-9_]*)*$`. Dots are namespace separators, but
  the complete event name remains one string.
- Values are `null`, booleans, finite IEEE-754 binary64 numbers, strings,
  arrays, and objects. There is no binary value type.
- Numbers use JSON lexical grammar; integer-valued binary64 results MUST be
  safe integers. Negative zero is significant.
- Strings use JSON escapes. The reference JavaScript string limit is measured
  after decoding in UTF-16 code units, not UTF-8 bytes or Unicode scalar values.
- Object keys use the lowercase identifier grammar; quoted keys, duplicate keys,
  comments, tuple syntax, and trailing commas are not ENF 1.0 features.
- Parsing rejects invalid input rather than returning a partial successful
  document. Resource-limit failures are distinct from syntax failures.
- `stringify()` validates in-process JavaScript values but is not a sandbox for
  hostile live objects or Proxy traps.
